result:
:::writing
The train arrived without ceremony, as if it had always been there and we had simply failed to notice it until now. One moment the platform was empty except for a stray newspaper turning its own pages in the wind, and the next there it stood, doors open, lights humming softly like a creature that breathes without lungs.

No one else seemed surprised. A man in a gray coat stepped aboard while finishing a sentence into his phone, as though the timing had been arranged in advance. A woman with a bright orange suitcase followed, pausing only to adjust her scarf. Even the pigeons barely reacted, shifting a few inches along the edge of the platform before settling again.

I hesitated. Not because I thought the train was dangerous, but because it felt deliberate in a way that unsettled me. There was no announcement, no destination listed above the doors, no conductor leaning out to shout last calls. It simply existed, and by existing, it seemed to be asking a question.

Or maybe it was answering one.

I stepped on.

Inside, the air was warmer than expected, carrying the faint smell of citrus and old paper. The seats were upholstered in a fabric that looked worn but not used, as though it had been aged artificially. The lighting was soft and indirect, making it difficult to tell where exactly it came from. There were passengers scattered throughout the car, each absorbed in their own small world.

A young boy sat across from me, turning a coin over and over in his fingers without ever dropping it. His eyes were fixed on the coin, but his expression suggested he wasn’t really seeing it. Next to him, an older woman read a book with no title on its cover. Every few seconds, she would nod slightly, as if agreeing with something written there.

I chose a seat by the window. The glass reflected my face back at me, but not quite accurately. My reflection seemed a fraction slower, its expressions lagging just enough to be noticeable. When I leaned closer, it leaned too—but a heartbeat later.

The train began to move.

There was no lurch, no shift in momentum. The platform simply slid away, like a scene being changed behind a stage curtain. Outside, the city blurred—not into streaks of color, but into impressions. Buildings became suggestions of buildings, streets became hints of movement. It was less like traveling through space and more like passing through ideas of places.

I glanced around to see if anyone else noticed. No one reacted. The man in the gray coat had ended his call and now stared at his reflection in the darkened window across from him. The woman with the orange suitcase had closed her eyes, her hands folded neatly on top of the handle.

“First time?” a voice asked.

I turned. The speaker sat diagonally across the aisle: a person whose age was difficult to determine. Their hair was dark but streaked with silver, their posture relaxed but not casual. They smiled—not broadly, but with a kind of quiet recognition.

“Yes,” I said.

They nodded, as if confirming something they already knew. “It’s always a little strange at first.”

“Where is this going?” I asked.

They considered the question, tilting their head slightly. “That depends on how you think about it.”

“That’s not very helpful.”

“It’s not meant to be unhelpful,” they said gently. “It’s just accurate.”

I leaned back in my seat. “Okay. Then how should I think about it?”

They smiled again, this time a little wider. “Like this: the train doesn’t take you somewhere new. It takes you somewhere true.”

I frowned. “That sounds like something from a self-help book.”

“Most true things do,” they replied.

I wasn’t sure whether to laugh.

The train passed through what looked like a tunnel, though “looked like” was doing most of the work. The walls were not solid; they shimmered, like heat rising from asphalt on a summer day. For a moment, I thought I saw shapes within the shimmer—faces, maybe, or memories—but they slipped away before I could focus on them.

“Do we choose where we get off?” I asked.

“Sometimes,” the person said. “Sometimes not.”

“That’s reassuring.”

“It shouldn’t be,” they said. “Choice is overrated.”

I raised an eyebrow. “You don’t believe that.”

“I believe people like the idea of choice more than the reality of it,” they said. “Too many options can make you stand still. Too few can make you desperate. This”—they gestured around the car—“finds a balance.”

I looked down at my hands. They were steady, but I felt a faint buzzing under my skin, like the anticipation before a storm. “And what happens if I don’t like where I end up?”

They shrugged lightly. “You will.”

“That’s a bold claim.”

“It’s an informed one.”

The train began to slow, though again there was no physical sensation of deceleration. It was more like the world outside decided to come into focus again. Shapes solidified. Colors deepened. The impression of a place became a place itself.

We pulled into a station that looked familiar in the way a dream can feel familiar—recognizable without being identifiable. The platform was lined with lamps that cast a soft, golden light. A sign hung overhead, but the letters shifted when I tried to read them, rearranging themselves into different words each time.

The doors opened.

No one moved at first. Then the boy with the coin stood up. He slipped the coin into his pocket and walked toward the exit without looking back. The older woman closed her book, nodded once more, and followed him.

I stayed seated.

“Aren’t you getting off?” the person across from me asked.

“I don’t know,” I admitted.

They studied me for a moment, not unkindly. “You’re waiting for certainty,” they said. “There isn’t any.”

“I figured.”

“Then what are you waiting for?”

I looked out at the platform. People were walking in different directions, each with a sense of purpose that didn’t seem rushed. No one appeared lost. No one seemed to hesitate.

“I guess I’m waiting to feel ready,” I said.

They nodded, as if that made perfect sense. “That feeling rarely arrives on its own.”

The doors chimed softly, a gentle warning.

“If I stay on?” I asked.

“Then you stay on,” they said. “There will be other stops.”

“And if I get off?”

“Then you get off.”

I laughed under my breath. “You’re not big on detailed explanations, are you?”

“They’re overrated too.”

The chime sounded again.

I stood.

For a moment, I just stood there, suspended between two simple options that felt anything but simple. Then, before I could think my way out of it, I stepped onto the platform.

The air was cooler than inside the train, carrying a scent I couldn’t quite place—something clean, with a hint of rain. The doors closed behind me with a soft, final sound.

I turned back.

The train was already gone.

Not departing, not fading—just gone, as if it had never been there at all.

I stood on the platform, alone but not lonely, uncertain but not afraid. The shifting sign above me settled, just for a moment, into letters I could read.

It didn’t name the place.

It simply said: “Here.”

And for the first time, that felt like enough.
:::writing

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
